paperTitle: Singapore-Cambridge GCE O-Level 1184 English Language — AI-authored Simplified Practice 25: Auntie Lim's Bee Hoon (§B style, simplified band)

sections.instruction: Read the narrative text below about a Secondary 1 boy who has eaten breakfast at the same hawker centre at Tampines for two years, and how he says goodbye to Auntie Lim at the bee hoon stall on his last morning before his family moves to Yishun. Answer the questions that follow. Section B [15 marks] — AI-authored simplified practice paper modelled on the Singapore-Cambridge 1184/02 (formerly 1128) Section B format and calibrated to the SEAB assessment objectives (literal / inferential comprehension, vocabulary in context, language for effect), pitched at students stretching from middle secondary toward the full O-Level standard. Original passage written for the morning quiz bank; not from a past exam paper. Items mix mark values [1]/[2]: 7 short-answer questions (Q1-Q7) plus a 4-blank emotion-flowchart MCQ (Q8). Answer in your own words as far as possible.
sections.passage:
Paragraph 1
For two years, I had eaten breakfast at the same hawker centre at Tampines every weekday before school. I would walk down from Block 410 with my school bag on my back, and by the time I sat down, Auntie Lim already knew what I wanted.

Paragraph 2
Her stall sold fried bee hoon. The signboard was old and the red paint had peeled. My order had never changed since Primary 5 — fried bee hoon, one fishcake, no chilli, more spring onion. Auntie Lim could recite it from memory every morning.

Paragraph 3
She was not a chatty auntie. She wore a faded blue apron and tied her hair in a small bun. When I came to the counter she would simply nod, scoop the bee hoon, place the fishcake on top, and add the extra spring onion without me saying a word.

Paragraph 4
In Sec 1, my father told us one evening that we were moving to Yishun. The new flat was bigger, he said. My MRT ride to school would be too long, so I would change schools next year. I nodded and did not say much. I thought, mostly, about the bee hoon.

Paragraph 5
My last day at the Tampines hawker centre was a Friday in November. I sat at our usual table near the drinks stall. When I went up to order, I did not know how to say what I wanted to say. So I just said, 'Auntie, last time today. I am moving to Yishun next week.'

Paragraph 6
Auntie Lim did not say much. She scooped the bee hoon as always. She placed the fishcake on top. Then, without looking up, she opened a small container and added a second fishball to my plate. She had never done that before. She pushed the plate across with one hand and took my coins with the other.

Paragraph 7
As I picked up the plate, she called after me. Her voice was a little louder than usual. 'You come back when you grown up, can?' I turned around and nodded. I could not find any other answer.

Paragraph 8
On the bus home that afternoon, I sat by the window and looked out at the HDB blocks passing by. I promised myself, quietly, that I would. One day, when I was grown up, I would come back. The extra fishball, I realised, had been a promise — and now mine was too.

(AI-authored original narrative for O-Level 1184 §B style practice, simplified-band difficulty; not from a past examination paper. Bee hoon: thin rice vermicelli noodles; fishcake and fishball: common accompaniments served at hawker centres.)

questions.stem: Q1. From Paragraph 2, what exactly did the narrator order for breakfast every morning? [1]
questions.answer: fried bee hoon, one fishcake, no chilli, more spring onion

questions.stem: Q2. What does the word 'recite' (Paragraph 2) suggest about how well Auntie Lim knew the narrator's order? [1]
questions.answer: she knew it perfectly, by heart / from memory

questions.stem: Q3. What does the phrase 'a faded blue apron' (Paragraph 3) suggest about Auntie Lim's stall? [1]
questions.answer: it was old / well-worn, used for a long time

questions.stem: Q4. Using your own words, explain why the narrator 'thought, mostly, about the bee hoon' when he heard the family was moving. (Paragraph 4) [2]
questions.answer: ① the daily breakfast was the part of his old life he cared about most; ② it stood for everything familiar he would lose by moving away

questions.stem: Q5. Using your own words, explain why Auntie Lim added a second fishball to the narrator's plate. (Paragraph 6) [2]
questions.answer: ① it was her quiet way of saying goodbye; ② since she was not chatty, she showed she would miss him through a small gift instead of words

questions.stem: Q6. Using your own words, explain how the narrator felt when Auntie Lim called after him, and why he could only nod. (Paragraph 7) [2]
questions.answer: ① he was moved / too emotional to speak; ② a nod was all he could manage because words were too hard at that moment

questions.stem: Q7. What is the effect of the dash in 'The extra fishball, I realised, had been a promise — and now mine was too' (Paragraph 8)? [2]
questions.answer: the dash pauses before the key idea, drawing attention to it; it links her unspoken promise to his and gives the ending a quiet, thoughtful close

sections.instruction: Q8. The narrator's main feeling shifts across the morning of his last day at the Tampines hawker centre. For each part of the passage, choose ONE word from the bank that best describes his dominant feeling at that moment. There are some extra words in the bank you do not need to use. Word bank: routine / habitual / sad / resolute / cheerful / bored / nervous / surprised. The original task carries 4 marks; each blank is split into its own MCQ so each sub-part can be auto-graded independently.
sections.passage: Refer to the same 'Auntie Lim's Bee Hoon' narrative in Exercise 1 above. The narrator's feelings shift through four stages — from a comfortable, ordinary routine in the first weeks and months, to a habitual sense of unspoken familiarity at the stall, to a quiet sadness on his last day when Auntie Lim adds the second fishball, ending in a resolute promise on the bus home that he will one day come back. Pick the single best word from the bank for each moment.

questions.stem: Q8(i). What best describes the narrator's dominant feeling in Paragraph 1 as he walks down from Block 410 each weekday to the same hawker centre at Tampines? [1]
Q8(ii). What best describes the narrator's dominant feeling in Paragraph 3 as Auntie Lim silently scoops the bee hoon and adds the extra spring onion without him having to say a word? [1]
Q8(iii). What best describes the narrator's dominant feeling in Paragraph 6 when Auntie Lim, without looking up, quietly adds a second fishball to his plate? [1]
Q8(iv). What best describes the narrator's dominant feeling in Paragraph 8 as he sits on the bus home and quietly promises himself that one day, when he is grown up, he will come back? [1]
